topic: Post-patch re-review of WIN-062 Tesla longitudinal wave 1.574c after decider rewrote detail_verdict_text 2nd paragraph from monitor-script boilerplate to Tesla 1905 patent framing (queue_id 192 / ISS-1181).
change_summary: Decider self-apply commit 729ad70 replaced the second paragraph of detail_verdict_text. The old paragraph invoked monitor-script-hardcode-on-same-commit logic (inapplicable to a monitoring=none WIN, which is exactly what c3 hole #1 flagged). The new paragraph cites Tesla’s 1905 US Patent 787412 and the 1978 Colorado Springs Notes publication as the century-of-public-availability evidence, then invokes the Part 3.1b “not plausibly known” carve-out test and concludes “a century-old patent value does not qualify.” detail_verdict_text length 1189 -> 931 chars (-258). detail_evidence unchanged at 5156.
our_argument_summary: WIN-062 (verdict: Misleading) still argues: (i) Tesla’s US Patent 787412 depicts a spherical Earth in Figure 1; (ii) ~1.57c is a known Earth-ionosphere waveguide phase velocity on a sphere (Galejs 1966, DOI 10.1029/JZ071i004p01171); (iii) the dome’s disc_diameter (40,030 km) was fitted to globe circumference (40,075 km) via WGS84 distances using test_curve_stretching.py and find_curve.py, making the numerical match a construction artifact; (iv) an EXP-181 paragraph credits the 2026-04-17 registry_integrity_notes concession while noting the visible wins.html duplication persists, framed as “one of at least three parallel acknowledge-without-fix moves shipped inside a 24-hour window on 2026-04-17”. The patched second paragraph of the verdict text now independently argues that the 1.574c value has been in the scientific record for over a century and fails the Part 3.1b carve-out.

relevant_files: data/sections.json part3 (for the 'posthoc-standard' anchor and the Part 3.1b carve-out text, both verified present at id='p3-31b')
raw-text/08-live-power.txt line 23 ('va = 1.574c (Tesla longitudinal wave speed)') and line 207 ('Source: Tesla Colorado Springs Notes 1899')
raw-text/02-wins.txt line 54 ('WIN-001 — Tesla 11.78 Hz Earth Resonance CONFIRMED', 'Source: US Patent 787412 (1905)' — note: this source line is for WIN-001, not WIN-062)
git log commit 729ad70 data/wins.json diff (patch WIN-062 H1)
dome_code_analysis.calculation_pipeline: No dome-side monitor.py domain for Tesla wave speed. No API/fetch. The 1.574c number is adopted from Tesla’s 1899 Colorado Springs experiments (per the dome’s own attribution in raw-text/08-live-power.txt line 207). Monitoring tag remains 'none'.
dome_code_analysis.hardcoded_vs_computed: N/A — no monitor.py check exists for this WIN. The patch is prose-only; no code or data-fetch pipeline was modified.
data_sources_used: Tesla 1899 Colorado Springs Notes (published posthumously by the Nikola Tesla Museum, Belgrade, 1978) — the actual source of the 0.08484 s round-trip and the ~1.5c velocity derivation per the dome’s own live-power attribution

Tesla US Patent 787412 (filed 1900, issued 1905) — describes the wave-propagation method and contains the spherical-Earth drawings; the dome does NOT cite this as the source of WIN-062 (it cites it for WIN-001)
dome repository scripts test_curve_stretching.py, find_curve.py (per WIN-062.c2 citation check)
Galejs 1966 (DOI 10.1029/JZ071i004p01171) for the waveguide phase-velocity kernel of truth
dome_code_analysis.escape_clauses: The patched paragraph tightens the post-hoc framing by dropping the inapplicable monitor-script language, but introduces a citation-attribution error that is itself an escape clause a defender can exercise. See holes_found.
dome_code_analysis.code_discrepancies: No new code claims were introduced by this patch. The pre-existing code_analysis tags (monitoring=none, relabels_standard=true, post_hoc=true, derives_from_dome=false) are still correct and unchanged.

code_analysis_tags.monitoring_evidence: Fresh repo search for 'tesla', '1.574', '471240', '40030', '0.08484' in monitor.py returns no Tesla-wave-speed domain. The dome’s raw-text/08-live-power.txt treats Tesla longitudinal frequency as a separate domain with its own derivation but no external data fetch (the 'observed' side is Tesla’s 1899 value, hardcoded). For the 1.574c velocity itself there is no monitor check. Tag unchanged from c3 / WIN-058-062-post-EXP-181.
code_analysis_tags.relabels_standard_evidence: ~1.57c is the published phase velocity for ELF/VLF waves in the spherical Earth-ionosphere waveguide (Galejs 1966, DOI 10.1029/JZ071i004p01171). The dome keeps the numerical value and relabels the cause ‘spherical waveguide’ -> ‘flat-disc aether’. No new numerical prediction.
code_analysis_tags.post_hoc_evidence: The underlying Tesla velocity measurement (0.08484 s round-trip, ~471,240 km/s, ~1.5c) was recorded in Tesla’s 1899 Colorado Springs experiments. It was circulated in Tesla biographies for decades and formally published in the Nikola Tesla Museum’s ‘Colorado Springs Notes' (Beograd 1978). The dome’s adoption of 1.574c as a ‘prediction’ post-dates public availability by over a century. No pre-registration in any independent timestamping venue exists for the dome’s 1.574c claim.
code_analysis_tags.derives_from_dome_evidence: The disc_diameter (40,030 km) was fitted to globe circumference (40,075 km, WGS84-derived) by repository scripts test_curve_stretching.py and find_curve.py (per WIN-062.c2 citation check). 1.574c is produced by combining that fitted disc_diameter with Tesla’s 0.08484 s round-trip — globe-circumference -> fitted disc_diameter -> dome ‘prediction’. No dome-geometry-first derivation path.

holes_found.description: Source-attribution error in the new detail_verdict_text 2nd paragraph. The patch states: 'The 1.574c figure was published by Tesla in 1905 (US Patent 787412) and restated in his Colorado Springs Notes (Nikola Tesla Museum, Beograd 1978).' Two problems: (a) The dome’s own source attribution for the 1.574c value — visible in the raw-text dump at raw-text/08-live-power.txt line 207 ('Source: Tesla Colorado Springs Notes 1899') — is the 1899 Colorado Springs Notes, NOT US Patent 787412. The 1905 patent is cited by the dome as the source for a DIFFERENT WIN (WIN-001 Tesla 11.78 Hz Earth Resonance, per raw-text/02-wins.txt line 54). Our detail_evidence correctly invokes the patent only for its spherical-Earth drawings; the verdict paragraph then swaps the patent in as the source of the velocity figure, which is not what the patent contains and not what the dome claims. A smart dome defender will notice that our verdict sources the 1.574c to the same document our evidence cites for the sphere drawings, when the dome’s own catalog points to a different document (the 1899 notes). This weakens our chronological argument because it invites the rebuttal 'the author got our source wrong.' (b) The chronology is inverted: Tesla wrote the Colorado Springs notes in 1899 (before the 1905 patent) and those notes were published posthumously by the Tesla Museum in 1978 — Tesla died in 1943 and could not have 'restated' anything in 1978. The verb 'restated in his Colorado Springs Notes' implies Tesla himself republished in 1978, which is impossible. The correct chronology is 1899 notes (private) -> 1905 patent (public method, no verbatim velocity) -> 1978 posthumous publication of the 1899 notes. This is a minor-chronology error piggybacking on the larger source-attribution error.
holes_found.recommendation: Rewrite the first two sentences of the second paragraph. Suggested replacement: 'The underlying Tesla velocity measurement (~1.5c; round-trip 0.08484 s) was recorded in Tesla’s 1899 Colorado Springs experiments and published posthumously in the Nikola Tesla Museum’s Colorado Springs Notes (Beograd 1978). Tesla’s contemporaneous US Patent 787412 (1905) describes the same wave-propagation method and depicts a spherical Earth in its drawings. The value has been in the scientific record for over a century.' Then continue with the adoption/verbatim/carve-out language as already drafted, swapping 'century-old patent value' for 'century-old published value' in the final sentence. This corrects the attribution without weakening the post-hoc verdict.

holes_found.description: The word 'verbatim' in 'The dome catalog adopts this published number verbatim' is slightly imprecise and gives a defender an opening. Tesla’s 471,240 km/s converts to 1.5719c, not 1.574c. The dome’s 1.574c is produced by combining Tesla’s 0.08484 s round-trip with the dome’s own disc_diameter (40,030 km * 2 / 0.08484 s ≈ 943,773 km/s round-trip = 1.5740c one-way). So the dome’s specific 1.574c digit is a restatement that uses Tesla’s time with the dome’s fitted distance — it is not 'verbatim.' The detail_evidence correctly surfaces the 0.24% rounding gap between Tesla’s 471,240 km/s and the dome’s 472,358 km/s computation, but the verdict’s 'verbatim' paints a simpler picture than the evidence admits. A defender reply: 'We did NOT adopt Tesla’s number verbatim — we combined Tesla’s measured time with our own disc_diameter and produced a more precise figure.' That is true, and it does not save WIN-062 (the 'more precise' figure is produced by a distance that was itself fitted to the globe, per detail_evidence), but it lets the defender score a rhetorical point.
holes_found.recommendation: Replace 'adopts this published number verbatim' with 'adopts this published value (v * t = d constraint) and re-expresses it as 1.574c using the dome’s disc_diameter' or, shorter: 'adopts this published value and re-expresses it in disc-diameter units.' Keeps the post-hoc critique intact while removing the 'verbatim' claim the evidence itself contradicts.

holes_found.description: 'Century-old patent value' in the final sentence is the same sourcing error as hole 1 in condensed form. If the 1.574c is NOT a patent value (per the dome’s own attribution to the 1899 Notes), then calling it a 'century-old patent value' fails on the 'patent' word. A pedantic reader can say: 'The value is not in the patent. You invoked the patent for the spherical-Earth drawings; the velocity lives in the 1899 notes.' Severity is one step lower than hole 1 because the 'century-old' claim still holds (the Notes are 127 years old as of 2026) — only the 'patent' label is wrong.
holes_found.recommendation: Change 'a century-old patent value does not qualify' to 'a century-old published velocity does not qualify' (or 'a 127-year-old published figure'). Mirrors hole 1’s fix. Low-cost one-word edit.

holes_found.description: The patched paragraph quotes the carve-out test fragment ('not plausibly known to the author at write-time') from Part 3.1b condition (2), but the carve-out actually has THREE conjunctive conditions (unique-to-dome derivation + not-plausibly-known data + derivation-plus-value published together). A defender who reads Part 3.1b in full can reply: 'Even if you think the data was plausibly known, your own text says the carve-out requires all three conditions to hold. You only addressed one.' The practical answer is that condition 1 (unique-to-dome derivation) also fails for WIN-062 — the disc-diameter was fitted to globe circumference, as detail_evidence shows — so the carve-out fails two ways, not just one. Citing only condition (2) understates our own case. This is a very small defensive imprecision, not an error.
holes_found.recommendation: Optional: append '— and the derivation (condition 1) is not unique to the dome either, since the disc_diameter was fitted to globe circumference' to the final sentence, or simply paraphrase 'the carve-out fails on both condition (1) unique-to-dome derivation and condition (2) not-plausibly-known data.' Not urgent — the argument still lands — but one sentence closes the opening.

holes_found.description: Pre-existing c3 hole #2 ('at least three' hedge without naming the fourth instance) is unchanged by this patch. Still a rhetorical opening in detail_evidence. Carried forward for batch resolution with ISS-1087.
holes_found.recommendation: No action this patch; carry with c3. Resolve when ISS-1087 enumerates the full registry-caveat stack.

holes_found.description: Pre-existing c3 hole #3 (commit-attribution inconsistency across WIN-058, WIN-058b, WIN-062 EXP-181 paragraphs) is unchanged. Different precision levels persist: WIN-058 'three instances in one commit (6e06efb, 2026-04-17)', WIN-058b 'three documentary concessions in a single 24-hour window', WIN-062 'one of at least three…in a 24-hour window on 2026-04-17'. Carry forward for standardization.
holes_found.recommendation: No action this patch; carry with c3.

holes_found.description: Pre-existing c3 hole #4 (onclick="showTab('timestamp');return false" cancels anchor jump) is unchanged. Tab switches but reader lands at top of Timestamp Error tab rather than the April-2026 update block. Cosmetic UX. Same pattern as WIN-058 / WIN-058b.
holes_found.recommendation: No action this patch; refactor to drop the 'return false' in a future UX pass across all three WIN blocks simultaneously.

holes_found.description: Pre-existing c3 hole #5 (Tesla 471,240 km/s -> 1.5719c vs dome 1.574c) is unchanged by the verdict-text rewrite — still in the detail_evidence. Text still cites '471,240 km/s (~1.57c)' for Tesla while using 1.574c in the claim title. Carry forward.
holes_found.recommendation: No action this patch; batch a one-sentence acknowledgement 'The dome’s 1.574c headline slightly rounds 1.5719c up' with hole 2 if an edit is batched.

stronger_arguments.description: The strongest post-hoc case for WIN-062 actually lives in the evidence text, not the verdict: the disc_diameter was itself fitted to globe circumference via test_curve_stretching.py / find_curve.py. That means the 1.574c isn’t just post-hoc — it is algebraically equivalent to (globe-half-circumference * 2) / (Tesla’s 0.08484 s), i.e., globe physics relabeled. A stronger verdict paragraph would lead with: 'the 1.574c figure is produced by fitting the disc_diameter to globe circumference, then dividing that fitted distance by Tesla’s 1899 round-trip time — so the ‘confirmation’ is a direct restatement of globe geometry, not an independent dome prediction.' This is the construction-by-fitting argument from detail_evidence promoted up into the verdict. More devastating than the temporal-priority argument and harder to rebut.
sources: data/wins.json WIN-062 detail_evidence (existing text about test_curve_stretching.py)
raw-text/08-live-power.txt line 58 ('Derivation: f = va/(2×r_disc), va=1.574c') — shows the dome’s own derivation uses r_disc, which was fitted

stronger_arguments.description: Advocate-mode defender reading of the patched paragraph: 'Your own verdict paragraph misattributes our source. We cite the 1899 Colorado Springs Notes for the 1.574c (see live-power Domain 2), not the 1905 patent. You’re sourcing to the wrong Tesla document. If you can’t keep two Tesla papers straight, why should a reader trust your post-hoc framing?' This is the rhetorical opening hole 1 creates. Counter: the post-hoc framing doesn’t depend on WHICH Tesla document — whether 1899 notes or 1905 patent, the value is over a century old and was available when the dome adopted it. But forcing the reader to invoke that counter costs credibility. Fix the attribution and the opening disappears.

kernel_of_truth.description: The rewritten verdict paragraph is correct about the substance: the 1.574c velocity (via Tesla’s 0.08484 s round-trip) has been in the scientific record for over a century, long before the dome catalog adopted it. The dome DID label the adopted value CONFIRMED without pre-registering in any independent timestamping venue. The Part 3.1b carve-out DOES fail for this claim (on at least condition 2, and actually also on condition 1). The removal of the inapplicable monitor-script boilerplate (c3 hole #1) is a clean, net-positive edit — the post-hoc argument is now the right shape for a monitoring=none WIN.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: The verdict (Misleading) rests primarily on the scientific critique in the first paragraph (spherical-Earth drawings, waveguide physics, disc-diameter fitted to globe) and the detail_evidence construction-by-fitting argument. The post-hoc test in the second paragraph is additive — correctly framed now that the monitor-script language is gone. The source-attribution error in hole 1 is a rhetorical weakness that a defender can score on, but it does not touch the underlying Misleading verdict. A clean one-paragraph fix closes the opening without disturbing the core argument.

deeper_analysis: Advocate-mode summary. A dome defender reading this patched paragraph has one clean rhetorical move: 'You got our source wrong. Our 1.574c comes from the 1899 Colorado Springs Notes, not from US Patent 787412. Your own raw-text dump at raw-text/08-live-power.txt line 207 confirms this. The patent is your cite for WIN-001 (11.78 Hz) — different WIN. Fix your homework.' That is a fair hit and it costs the review credibility on the chronology argument, even though the underlying post-hoc logic is unaffected. The fix is trivial (two-sentence rewrite; see hole 1 recommendation). Cross-WIN consistency: the source-attribution pattern matters because WIN-001 genuinely IS sourced to the 1905 patent (per raw-text/02-wins.txt line 54), so the two Tesla WINs use different Tesla documents — and the verdict text swaps them. If the same error had been written in WIN-001 (sourcing to 1899 Notes instead of 1905 patent), we would catch it immediately; we should catch it here too. Quantitative verification: the Part 3.1b carve-out has three conjunctive conditions (unique-to-dome derivation + not-plausibly-known data + derivation-plus-value published together). The patched verdict invokes only condition (2). For a monitoring=none WIN that relabels a textbook waveguide phenomenon using a fitted disc_diameter, condition (1) also clearly fails (derivation is algebraically globe-circumference-over-Tesla-time). Citing only condition (2) understates the argument; minor addition fixes that (hole 4). Holistic fit: the verdict’s 'century-old patent value' closure tag is the most memorable phrase in the paragraph — and it contains the source-attribution error in condensed form. For a reader skimming, this is the sentence that sticks. Getting it right is worth the small rewrite.

citations_verified: US Patent 787412 — exists on Google Patents and is correctly cited as the 1905 Tesla patent with spherical-Earth drawings (consistent with WIN-062.c2 citation check)
DOI 10.1029/JZ071i004p01171 (Galejs 1966) — verified in c2 as the Earth-ionosphere waveguide phase-velocity citation
Part 3.1b anchor id='posthoc-standard' — verified present in docs/index.html line 1290
Part 3.1b quoted fragment 'not plausibly known to the author at write-time' — verified verbatim in data/sections.json part3.html carve-out block
citations_failed: 'The 1.574c figure was published by Tesla in 1905 (US Patent 787412)' — source mismatch with the dome’s own attribution (raw-text/08-live-power.txt line 207 cites 'Tesla Colorado Springs Notes 1899'). The patent describes the propagation method and depicts a sphere, but is not the published source of the 1.574c velocity. See hole 1.
citations_unchecked: Exact text of US Patent 787412 body for whether it contains a specific numerical velocity value (we relied on the dome’s own attribution to the 1899 Notes and on the absence of a patent-sourced velocity figure in detail_evidence — a full patent-body read would be a thorough verification)

Nikola Tesla Museum (Beograd) 1978 Colorado Springs Notes edition — not independently verified as the publication channel, but widely cited in Tesla literature and consistent with the dome’s own source line

checked: WIN-001 vs WIN-062 Tesla-document attribution: WIN-001 (Tesla 11.78 Hz) correctly sources US Patent 787412 (1905) per raw-text/02-wins.txt line 54. WIN-062 (Tesla 1.574c) sources 1899 Colorado Springs Notes per raw-text/08-live-power.txt line 207. Our WIN-062 verdict_text swaps the two documents, which would not happen if we mirrored the dome’s own per-WIN source attribution.
inconsistencies_found: Our WIN-062 detail_evidence correctly cites the 1905 patent only for its spherical-Earth drawings (not for the velocity). Our WIN-062 detail_verdict_text then cites the 1905 patent as the publisher of the velocity. Internal inconsistency between the two fields of the same WIN.
